# ADR-260029: Network View Circle Node and Connector Interaction Baseline

## Workflow

- This ADR records the accepted visual grammar for the Network view.
- It defines node shape, connector affordance, and edge anchoring behavior so the network representation behaves like a graph database explorer rather than a flow diagram.
- This ADR refines ADR-260028 and visual usage from ADR-260015.

Status: Accepted
Date: 2026-05-12
Owners: Repository owner, product collaborator

## Context

The new Network view needs a concrete interaction model that is distinct from the Flow view while still using the same underlying dataset.

The desired behavior is Neo4j-like: atoms appear as circles, a small side connector acts as the drag/edge creation affordance, and an established relationship should anchor to the nearest edge of the source and target circles once the connector touches another atom.

## Decision

### D1: Circle Node Shape
- Network-view atoms render as circles.
- Circles are the primary node shape for the Network view MVP.

### D2: Side Connector Affordance
- Each circle exposes a small connector dot on its side.
- The connector dot is the handle the user can drag to begin an edge/relationship creation gesture.

### D3: Edge Anchoring Behavior
- When the dragged connector touches another atom, the relationship is established between the two atoms.
- The rendered edge anchors to the nearest edge point of each circle, matching the Neo4j-style interaction model.

### D4: Shared Dataset Semantics
- This connector-and-anchor grammar applies only to the Network view representation.
- It does not change the underlying atom/bond data model or the Flow view semantics.

## Alternatives Considered

- Option A: Use rectangular or freeform nodes. Rejected: weaker alignment with the requested graph-database style.
- Option B: Use center-to-center edge attachment only. Rejected: loses the intended Neo4j-like node boundary behavior.
- Option C: Hide the connector affordance until hover. Rejected for MVP: reduces discoverability of edge creation.

## Consequences

### Positive
- Network view will feel immediately familiar to users used to graph database tools.
- Circle nodes simplify recognition and make edge anchoring visually consistent.
- Connector affordance makes relationship creation more discoverable.

### Negative
- Edge anchoring logic is more specific than a generic graph renderer.
- Small connectors may require careful hit-area tuning for usability.

### Risks
- If the connector dot is too small, drag initiation may feel imprecise.
- If anchor snapping is too strict, edge creation may become frustrating on dense graphs.

## Verification

- Verify Network view renders circle nodes.
- Verify each node exposes a visible connector dot/handle.
- Verify dragging from the connector can create a relationship with another node.
- Verify established edges anchor to the nearest circle boundary points.
- Verify Flow view behavior remains unchanged.

## Rollback

- Roll back D2/D3 to generic node-edge interaction if drag precision or renderer complexity becomes a blocker.
- Retain circle nodes even if connector affordance needs simplification.
